Table 1: Performance comparison on four datasets.

Figure 1: The overall framework of the proposed LTE-ODE. (Left) Input & Initialization: The input data X is projected via Winput and concatenated with node embeddings Enode to form initial dual-stream states. (Middle) Continuous-Discrete Evolution Loop: Over S steps, a dual-solver (Euler and RK2) evaluates the continuous vector field fθ. The numerical discrepancy between them yields the Local Truncation Error (LTE) Et, which generates a spatial attention mask Mt. This mask dynamically activates the discrete shock compensator gϕ to inject high-frequency momentum into the continuous trajectory. (Right) Prediction: Evolved features are concatenated and passed through a regression layer. Key Take-away: The LTE serves as an unsupervised, physics-informed bridgeit gracefully maintains pure continuous ODE evolution in smooth traffic regions while forcefully activating discrete compensations specifically at nodes experiencing abrupt shocks.
